%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# 1.2-3 Time Reversal

> **Section Objective:** Learn how time reversal creates \(x(-t)\) by reflecting a signal about the vertical time axis.

## Concepts In This Section

- time reversal
- vertical-axis reflection
- \(x(-t)\)
- time-reversal vs amplitude reversal
- support interval reversal

## 1. Time Reversal Means Left-Right Reflection

Think of the graph of \(x(t)\) as a rigid wire frame hinged at the vertical axis \(t=0\). Time reversal swings that frame 180° about the hinge. Every event that happened at time \(t\) now appears at time \(-t\), while the amplitude value is completely unchanged.

For example, if a peak of height 4 occurred at \(t=3\), after time reversal that same peak of height 4 appears at \(t=-3\). The graph is mirrored left-to-right, not flipped upside down.

### EXAM TIP

The trigger phrase is: **replace \(t\) by \(-t\), then mirror the graph about the vertical axis.**

## 2. Worked example: reversing an exponential signal

This is the core time-reversal formula.

- \(x(t)\) — the original signal
- \(\phi(t)\) — the time-reversed signal
- \(-t\) — the reflected time input: every time location changes sign

**When to use it:** Whenever a problem asks for the time-reversed signal, or asks you to sketch \(x(-t)\).

**Common misuse:** Do not flip the graph across the horizontal axis. Flipping up-down creates \(-x(t)\), not \(x(-t)\). The minus sign is inside the argument, not outside the function.

$$\phi(t) = x(-t)$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="book_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
![Figure 1.8](/figures/page-076-figure_1_8-1.png)
*Figure 1.8 shows that \(x(-t)\) is obtained by reflecting \(x(t)\) about the vertical \(t=0\) axis.*
<div class="lesson-figure-description">The figure contains two panels. Panel (a) shows the original signal \(x(t)\): a slanted segment from \(t=0\) to \(t=5\) and a curved segment in negative time. Panel (b) shows \(\phi(t)=x(-t)\): the mirror image of the same waveform about the vertical axis \(t=0\). Every feature that appears at time \(t\) in panel (a) appears at time \(-t\) in panel (b). The waveform height is fully preserved; only the time locations change sign. This figure is the canonical concept anchor for time reversal.</div>

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

## 2. Worked Example: Reversing an Exponential Signal

In Example 1.5, the original signal \(x(t) = e^{t/2}\) is nonzero on \(-5 \le t \le -1\) and zero elsewhere.

**Step 1 — Replace \(t\) by \(-t\) in the formula:**
$$e^{t/2} \xrightarrow{\;t\,\to\,-t\;} e^{-t/2}$$

**Step 2 — Reverse the support interval:**
The original interval \([-5, -1]\) becomes \([1, 5]\) because each endpoint changes sign and the order is restored so the smaller value comes first.

**Exam shortcut:** If a signal is nonzero on \([a, b]\), then \(x(-t)\) is nonzero on \([-b, -a]\).

#### Warning
Students often replace \(t\) by \(-t\) in the formula correctly but then forget to reverse the interval. Both steps are required.

$$x(-t) = \begin{cases} e^{-t/2}, & 1 \le t \le 5 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="book_image" data-teaching-role="example_support" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
![Fig. 1.9](/figures/page-077-fig__1_9-1.png)
*Fig. 1.9 shows an exponential signal on negative time becoming its time-reversed version on positive time.*
<div class="lesson-figure-description">The figure has two time-domain panels. Panel (a) shows \(x(t) = e^{t/2}\) on the interval from \(t = -5\) to \(t = -1\), with the signal zero outside that range and vertical jumps at the endpoints marking the support boundaries. Panel (b) shows \(x(-t) = e^{-t/2}\) on the interval from \(t = 1\) to \(t = 5\), again with vertical endpoint jumps. The graph is mirrored across \(t = 0\): the support moves from negative time to positive time, and the rising exponential in (a) becomes a decaying exponential in (b). Students should notice the graph is not flipped vertically — only the time axis is mirrored.</div>

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Time reversal formula: \(\phi(t) = x(-t)\) — replace \(t\) by \(-t\) everywhere in the signal.
- Point mapping: every \((t,\, x(t))\) moves to \((-t,\, x(t))\) — amplitude is always preserved.
- Interval rule: if \(x(t)\) is nonzero on \([a, b]\), then \(x(-t)\) is nonzero on \([-b, -a]\).
- Critical distinction: \(x(-t) \ne -x(t)\) — time reversal mirrors left-right, not up-down.

*Next, we will use these signal operations together when sketching and interpreting transformed signals.*
